lesson.menu_title: The Job Interview -- Telling Your Story
lesson.subtitle: Aula 15 -- The Job Interview -- Telling Your Story
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Rafael Pelizaro | Aula 15 -- The Job Interview

listenings.text: Thank you for the chance to talk with you today. My name is Rafael, and I am an IT project manager based in Sao Paulo. I have worked in technology for about twelve years, and I have led delivery teams for the last six. Let me tell you a little about my track record. Three years ago, I joined my current company to fix a project that was already late. I rebuilt the plan, I aligned the stakeholders, and we shipped the product on time. Since then, I have managed eight major releases, and I have never missed a launch date. My strengths are clear communication and staying calm under pressure. Last year, for example, we had a serious outage at night, and I led the response from the first alert to the postmortem. I have also mentored two junior managers, and one of them was promoted last quarter. I am applying for this role because I want a bigger challenge and a team I can grow. That is my story so far, and I am happy to go deeper into any part of it.

Hi Rafael, this is Sofia, the recruiter, with some quick feedback after your interview. First, thank you for your time today. The hiring panel was impressed. They liked that you have a strong track record and that you gave real examples instead of general statements. When they asked about a difficult moment, you talked about the outage you led last year, and that answer landed very well. One small tip for next time: you sometimes mixed the present perfect and the past simple. Remember, use the past simple when you say a finished time, like last year or three years ago, and use the present perfect for your experience up to now, like I have led eight releases. We have moved you to the shortlist, and you are one of three candidates. The next step is a final conversation with the engineering director on Thursday. Please prepare two questions for them, and be ready to walk through one project from start to finish. Well done, and talk soon.

extra_audio.text: First, research the company and the role, so you can show why you are a good fit. Next, prepare three short stories from your experience that prove your strengths. Then, in the interview, give clear examples and use real numbers from your track record. After that, ask two or three smart questions about the team and the work. Finally, send a short thank-you message and confirm the next step with the recruiter.
